coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient fluorescent probes from chitosan-based amino-functional carbon dots for the selective detection of Cu2+ traces
coredata.dc:creator: Trung, Le Gia
Subedi, Subhangi
Dahal, Bipeen
Truong, Phuoc Loc
Gwag, Jin Seog
Tran, Nguyen Tien
Nguyen, Minh Kim
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been rising intensely as a new fluorescent nanomaterial in recent years with their outstanding features for various potential applications. In this respect, the blue-green fluorescence CDs are successfully synthesized via the one-step simple and green carbonization method using chitosan as a carbon precursor. The as-prepared CDs hold the beneficially functional groups of amines on the surface with a high quantum yield of 6.28% under an excitation of 330 nm, and they are employed for fluorescently sensing copper(II) ions. Additionally, the CDs have excellent stability both in ambient conditions and even under the continuous irradiation of UV light (365 nm) for 1.5 h. Experimental results show that the as-synthesized nanometric particles have a nearly monodisperse and quasi-spherical structure with an average diameter of 4.9 nm and thickness of 5.1 nm, moreover, they exhibit very high fluorescence sensitivity and effective selectivity when the existence of Cu2+ traces. The Cu2+ detection depends almost on their dispersion rate and pH value in initial solutions with the detection limit as low as 105.7 nM within a linear wide dynamic range from 0 to 100 μM, which could offer a useful platform for identifying the Cu2+ traces in industrial wastewater or solutions. In addition, amino-functional CDs are successfully used to detect Cu2+ in a mixed solution of different metallic salts, that demonstrate good selectivity and provide promising applications for biosensing, imaging, or disease diagnosis.

(a) Full XPS spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of the (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s of the obtained NH2-CDs.

(a) FTIR spectra of NH2-CDs without and with adding Cu2+. (b) UV–Vis absorption (Abs), excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of the fluorescent NH2-CDs dispersed in water, with inset photographs showing this aqueous CDs solution: under ambient light (1), and 365 nm UV light with (2) and without (3) the presence of Cu2+. FM images of the aqueous NH2-CDs solution in a cell (gap of 10 μm) under illuminations of (c) 365 nm and (d) 405 nm: (i) bare NH2-CDs, (ii) initial NH2-CDs added Cu2+, and (iii) NH2-CDs added Cu2+ after 15 days.
(a) PL emission spectra of NH2-CDs in H2O under various excitation wavelengths from 290 to 390 nm with 20 nm increment. (b) The calculation plot for quantum yield.

The photostability of 50 μg mL−1 NH2-CDs in the different influences: (a) radiation time, (b) storage time, (c) NaCl with the different concentrations, and (d) six different buffers. The PL intensity is recorded at 430 nm with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.
Fluorescent intensity changes of the aqueous NH2-CDs solution (50 μg mL−1) at different pH values without and with adding Cu2+. The PL intensity is recorded at 430 nm.

(a) Emission spectra of NH2-CDs (50 μg mL−1) with different concentrations of Cu2+ from 0 to 400 μM. (b) The dependence between (F0/F ‒ 1) of NH2-CDs at 430 nm and the concentration of Cu2+, inset: Stern-Volmer plot of Cu2+-induced signal response showing a linear range from 0 to 100 μM.

The PL selectivity response of Cu2+ in the as-prepared aqueous NH2-CDs solution with different surveys: (a) individual metal ions (50 μM for each ion), (b) blend of metal ions (c) individual amino acids (i.e., amine), (50 μM for each amino acid), and (d) mixture of amino acids. The concentration of each metal ion and amino acid is 50 μM. The fluorescent intensity in panels (a) and (c) is recorded at 430 nm with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient fluorescent probes from chitosan-based amino-functional carbon dots for the selective detection of Cu2+ traces

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been rising intensely as a new fluorescent nanomaterial in recent years with their outstanding features for various potential applications. In this respect, the blue-green fluorescence CDs are successfully synthesized via the one-step simple and green carbonization method using chitosan as a carbon precursor. The as-prepared CDs hold the beneficially functional groups of amines on the surface with a high quantum yield of 6.28% under an excitation of 330 nm, and they are employed for fluorescently sensing copper(II) ions. Additionally, the CDs have excellent stability both in ambient conditions and even under the continuous irradiation of UV light (365 nm) for 1.5 h. Experimental results show that the as-synthesized nanometric particles have a nearly monodisperse and quasi-spherical structure with an average diameter of 4.9 nm and thickness of 5.1 nm, moreover, they exhibit very high fluorescence sensitivity and effective selectivity when the existence of Cu2+ traces. The Cu2+ detection depends almost on their dispersion rate and pH value in initial solutions with the detection limit as low as 105.7 nM within a linear wide dynamic range from 0 to 100 μM, which could offer a useful platform for identifying the Cu2+ traces in industrial wastewater or solutions. In addition, amino-functional CDs are successfully used to detect Cu2+ in a mixed solution of different metallic salts, that demonstrate good selectivity and provide promising applications for biosensing, imaging, or disease diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper(II) ions (Cu2+) play an important role in the physiological and biochemical regulations of intracellular electron transports, numerous enzymatic procedures, and various biological reactions [1]. In addition, Cu2+ is an essential micronutrient in many metabolic processes, as a cofactor for enzymes. Nonetheless, an insufficiency of copper can impair the activity of the enzymes and lead to a variety of neurological diseases in the human body, such as Alzheimer's disease [2]. Additionally, in case of excess, copper can become extremely toxic and dangerous to human health, which further causes abnormal metabolism and neurodegenerative changes, also the significant damage to important cellular organs in an organism [3,4]. As is expected, a technique along with the high sensitivity and selectivity to monitor the concentrations of copper ions for providing the efficiency of early diagnosis and also the prevention of disease should have been deeply studied.

Different analytical tools such as atomic absorption/mass spectrometry [5,6], ion chromatography [7], and electrochemical/potentiometric/fluorescent sensors [8,9], have been used to detect Cu2+ in recent years. Bio-imaging with highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensors gave an appealing approach to intracellularly monitoring copper ions in comparison to these conventional detection systems [10]. Significant efforts have gone into developing fluorescent sensors that use biological and organic molecules, or inorganic nanomaterials [11]. However, these strategies are also limited according to the significant biological toxicity, complexity, and cost of the sample preparation. Therefore, the potential introduction of effectively fluorescent nanosensors that have good water-solubility and proper biocompatibility for detecting heavy metal or Cu2+ traces in wastewater is a critical development today.

The nanostructured materials and their composite polymers have been widely studied in the last decade along with diverse potential applications such as two-dimensional inorganic films [12], gas separation [13,14], optical materials [15], or flame-resistant materials [16], etc. Additionally, the carbon-based nanomaterials could be employed in many applications in recent years, including the shear-aligned graphene oxide layers [17], supercapacitors [18,19], batteries [20], photocatalysts [21,22], bio-imaging [23], membranes [24,25], and sensors [26]. Carbon quantum dots, a member of the family of nanocarbon materials, have received much attention because of their unique properties [27]. Their outstanding characteristics such as the distinctive optics and electronics, water-solubility, excellent stability/photostability, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and low production cost [28], make them promising candidates for use in bio-modeling or sensors [29,30]. Additionally, the carbon quantum dots could also be switched by electric fields to exhibit linearly polarized photoluminescence from common liquid crystals for usefully optical applications [31]. A huge number of studies about carbon dots (CDs) have recently been focused on the effective detection of trace ions via their excellent fluorescence properties. The accidental yet fortunate discovery of CDs by Xu et al. (early 2000) [32] widely opened the motives of exploration and the utilization of these carbon nanoparticles. Liu et al. (2012) [33] first developed blue-emitting CDs with a wide detection range for Cu2+ detection from the plant-using hydrothermal process. Zong et al. (2014) produced CDs for sensing Cu2+ and l-Cysteine by using an “off-on” approach [9]. In addition, Zhu et al. (2012) [34] designed a dual-emission nanohybrid based on ratiometric fluorescent CDs. Liu et al. (2014) [35] also introduced a dual-emission fluorescent nanoprobe by using the surface functionalization of Rhodamine B doped silica nanoparticles which was applied in the Cu2+ detection and imaging. Dong et al. (2012) [36] also developed the fluorescent CDs that were polyamine-functionalized and utilized to detect the Cu2+ traces in river water. Although the CDs applied in the recognition of the Cu2+ traces have made some advances and shortcomings such as the complicated sample preparation and they remained unchanged the biological toxicity, also limited their further applications.

The amino groups on the surface of CDs play a vital role in attaching with Cu2+, resulting in a rapid fluorescent quenching and selectivity of the CDs-Cu2+ complexes [36]. In this context, an efficient recognition by extra-adding the amino groups has been designed for the low-dose detection of the Cu2+ traces in order to further improve their capability of sensing. In this study, a low-cost carbonization process was employed to simply synthesize amino-functionalized CDs (NH2-CDs). The obtained CDs were prepared from bulk chitosan which was automatically and simultaneously grafted by amino groups without further surface functionalization [27]. The NH2-CDs were utilized to detect Cu2+ which suggested a promising approach in the early prediction of disease diagnosis, followed by the discussion on the results. The results were demonstrated by employing TEM, fluorescent microscopy, optical measurements (UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy), and material characterizations (AFM, XPS, FTIR, XRD, and Raman).

All chemicals were at analytical grade and used as received. Bulk chitosan was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China. Dialysis membranes (MWCO = 1000 Da), amino acids, buffer solutions, and inorganic chemicals were received from Sigma Aldrich. Doubly distilled deionized water (DI, 18 MΩ) was employed for all experiments.

The CDs were prepared by using the carbonization process of chitosan as described in the literature [27]. In comparison with other methods, this method needs only simple one-step and gets the excellent products which are suitable for the large-scale production. Briefly, 1.5 g of bulk chitosan was carbonized at 300 °C for 2h (heating rate of 5 °C/min) under a nitrogen atmosphere (Scheme 1 ). The black products were collected and then mechanically powdered after being cooled down to room temperature. The collected powder of CDs was dispersed in distilled DI water, then filtrated and dialyzed for 3 days. Finally, the resultant products were freeze-dried to obtain the final CDs.

The characteristic spectra and apparatus were acquired by the following modes: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a microscope (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan). The atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was obtained with a scanning probe microscope (Multimode-8 Bruker, Germany). The samples used for AFM characterization were deposited on silicon wafers. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a spectrometer (Kα excitation radiation, Thermo Scientific, USA). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) was conducted using attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Scientific, USA) in a range of 500–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements on dried powder were carried out at room temperature on an M18XHF-SRA system (Mac Science, Japan) equipped with an Inel position detector with 2θ up to 120°. The used radiation is monochromatic Cu Kα (λ = 0.154 nm). Raman measurement was performed with a laser excitation wavelength of 633 nm using an Andor Monora Raman spectroscope (500i, UK). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometric data were recorded by using Cary 50-UV Vis spectrophotometers (Varian Inc., USA). Fluorescent microscopic (FM) images were taken by a fluorescent microscope (Olympus BX51, Korea). The excitation and emission of CDs were recorded by a Synergy H-1M fluorescence spectrometer (BioTek Instruments, Korea).

To prepare the neutral solutions for the fluorescent measurements of NH2-CDs, all of the fluorescence detection was performed in phosphate-buffered saline solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH = 7.4). PBS was used to disperse 2 mL of aqueous CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) to achieve 20 mL dispersion (with concentration of CDs: 50 μg mL−1). Different amounts of Cu(NO3)2 (i.e., Cu2+) were added to 20 mL of above dispersion to obtain the NH2-CDs-Cu2+ solution with the desired concentrations of Cu2+ in a range from 0.01 to 400 μM. The solutions were magnetically stirred for 5 min then sonicated mildly for 2 min and following incubated for 5 min at room temperature before doing the photoluminescence (PL) measurements. The fluorescence determination was conducted in a quartz cuvette with the excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths of 330 and 430 nm, respectively. Given that other metallic ions, amino acids, and pH values may cause the undesired noises to the Cu2+ detection and they affect the specificity of CDs. Some common metal ions (Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Co2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, and Zn2+) from their nitrate salts such as Fe(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, KNO3, NaNO3, Co(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Mn(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, AgNO3, and Zn(NO3)2; amino acids including Glycine (Gly), Asparagine (Asp), Phenylalanine (Phe), Histidine (His), Valine (Val), Threonine (Thr), Alanine (Ala), Cysteine (Cys), Tryptophan (Trp), Leucine (Leu), and Glutamine (Gln); and the different pH values (a range of 3–10) were prepared to further evaluate the selective detection of NH2-CDs for the different impacts. All metal ions and amino acids were also added to the aqueous CDs-PBS dispersions following the above Cu2+-adding step. The effect of pH on the PL of as-obtained CDs, in typical, 20 mL of such CDs-PBS dispersion (50 μg mL−1 of CDs) is adjusted to the target pH values by adding concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4) or sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution.

The experiments were all performed in triplicate. The average value of three separate iterations was used to compute the findings. For each case, the standard errors were represented by an error bar. In the analysis of variance, one-way ANOVA was employed, using Origin 2019 software, to determine the difference between the sample means. A p < 0.05 value is regarded to be a significant difference.

The TEM image in Fig. 1 a indicates that the as-synthesized CDs are nearly monodisperse with a short-range in diameter from 2.5 to 7.5 nm. The size distribution calculated by analyzing approximately 100 particles in TEM images (inset in Fig. 1a) shows an average size of 4.9 ± 2.5 nm. Fig. 1b exhibits the high resolution TEM image of an individual quantum nanoparticle with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is matching to the (100) facet of graphite [37]. To demonstrate the spherical morphology of CDs, AFM was taken to measure the thickness of particles (i.e., diameter in a vertical direction). Fig. 1c and d shows that the thickness of CDs is uniform (∼5.1 nm on average) and rather coincides with its diameter. XRD spectrum (Fig. 1e) in a wide range of 2θ diffraction angles is used to confirm that the obtained material is CDs. After the carbonization process, the spectrum of CDs displays a stretched-out peak at 2θ = 18.9° corresponding to the (002) facet of graphite [27,38]. Furthermore, the Raman spectrum of the NH2-CDs (Fig. 1f) reveals two large frequency bands at 1335 (D) and 1576 cm−1 (G). They are attributed to the oscillations in deformed graphite of sp3 carbon (D peak, defects of material) and the sp2 hybridization of sp2 carbon (G peak, oscillation of sp2 carbons) [39]. Since the intensity ratio of D and G bands (I D/I G) is high about ∼1.05, indicating that it is correlated to more defects and amorphous carbon with the disorder nature of CDs in a poor crystalline framework [27]. Fig. 2 a illustrates the full-scan XPS spectrum of the as-prepared NH2-CDs, which displays three distinct peaks of C1s (located at 285.18 eV), N1s (located at 400.21 eV), and O1s (located at 531.98 eV). It is definitely recognized that the apparent and sharp peak of N1s contributed to the rich of amine groups [40]. The C1s peak could be resolved into three component peaks (Fig. 2b) including C–C/CC (at 284.55 eV), C–O/C–N (at 285.76 eV), and CO/CN (at 287.81 eV) [27]. Meanwhile, this partial CO/CN band is lower [27]. In addition, the bands for the C–O and CO are also observed in the O1s peak (Fig. 2c) [41]. The N1s peak (Fig. 2d) reveals a clear peak at 399.98 eV corresponding to the N–H bond [40]. Because these findings were obtained from the XPS spectra, it is needless to further modify the surface of as-synthesized CDs due to the intrinsic existence of hydrophilic amino-functional groups. Therefore, these CDs could be well-dispersible in polar solvents (e.g., water, ethanol) [27]. Since it is well-known that the fluorescent behavior and quantum yield (QY) depend almost on the used solvents [42]. DI water is successfully used in this paper for simplifying the influence factors.

Fig. 3 a illustrates the FTIR spectra of NH2-CDs with and without the addition of Cu2+. The O–H and N–H stretching vibrations in the region of 3550–3350 cm−1 are attributed to the hydroxyl and amine groups of CDs. Very weak peaks at 2915 and 2890 cm−1 relate to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the C–H bond, respectively. The basic peaks of NH2-CDs locate at 1657 and 1591 cm−1 corresponding to N–H bending, 1461–1240 cm−1 corresponding to CC, CN, and CC–O groups [40], and the entirely disappeared vibrations of C–H bending in pyranose-ring (located at 1151–985 cm−1) due to the decomposition of chitosan after the carbonization process [27]. In addition, the peaks (located at 3389 and 1591 cm−1) are the vibrations of N–H bending, which is attributed to the presence of the surface-grafted amine groups [33]. These CDs own the hydrophilic oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups, leading to highly dispersible solutions in water [40]. In contrast, the FTIR spectrum of NH2-CDs when the addition of Cu2+ (NH2-CDs-Cu2+) shows a weak relocation and slight change in absorption intensities. Major other absorption peaks did not have any apparent changes (Fig. 3a). That demonstrated that the structure of CDs when the presence of Cu2+ (i.e., copper complexes) was affected insignificantly.

Generally, the absorbance characteristics of carbon-core-based materials identify at two regions of the π–π* and n–π* electronic transitions from UV–Vis to near-infrared regions [43]. Indeed, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NH2-CDs in Fig. 3b centers mainly at a wavelength of 260 nm, which is assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains (i.e., CC bonds) from the carbon core [44], however, it further 20 nm red-shifts when the addition of Cu2+. It is well-known that CDs-based solutions exhibit a typical excitation-wavelength-dependent PL when CDs are dissolved in various solvents [45,46]. Afterward, it is seen that the as-prepared aqueous CDs show a blue color under 365 nm UV irradiation and display excellent dispersibility in water (inset in Fig. 3b). From the CDs dispersion, a strong PL emission peak located at 430 nm is recorded under an excitation at 330 nm. Fig. 3b also reveals the excitation and emission spectra of aqueous CDs solutions with and without the addition of 50 μM Cu2+. Although the peak intensities of excitation and emission decrease significantly when adding Cu2+ into the CDs solution, their positions are unchanged. That reveals the fluorescent feature of CDs is changed when the presence of Cu2+, in addition, amine groups could make coordination compounds with copper ions by •NH2 ligands. Therefore, the fluorescent quenching mechanism almost caused by grafting Cu2+ in –NH2 groups on the surface of CDs is revealed clearly. Meanwhile, the optical nature of CDs under UV light (strong blue emission) will be immediately extinct when adding enough amounts of copper ions (inset in Fig. 3b).

These results are also confirmed by using the fluorescent microscope with two UV/Vis irradiations of 365 nm (Figs. 3c) and 405 nm (Fig. 3d). To demonstrate the fluorescent changes of the CDs and CDs-Cu2+ samples over time, a laboratory-made cell (2 × 2 cm2) was prepared by using two flat glass substrates and the distance between the two substrates was maintained using the polyacrylate film as a spacer with a gap of ∼10 μm. Then, a periphery of the cell was completely sealed by grease and epoxy glue to prevent water evaporation for checking within 15 days. A similar result to the PL measurements, the fluorescent behaviors turn obviously after quenching by Cu2+ (Fig. 3c–ii and 3d-ii). Moreover, the FM images show that Cu2+-quenched CDs are rather stable over time (more than 2 weeks), meanwhile, there is no decomposition between copper ions and CDs (Fig. 3c–iii and 3d-iii).

The phenomenon of excitation-dependent emission is clearly observed in Fig. 4 a. When the excitation wavelengths increase from 290 to 330 nm and 350–390 nm with the increment of 20 nm, the PL intensities remarkably increase and decrease respectively, while the emission-peak position is negligibly red-shifted. The excitation-dependent emission is common behavior of blue/green/red-emitting carbon quantum dots, and as is well-known, this feature is attributed to the size of non-uniform nanoparticles or the distribution of different surface states [44,47]. Although the mechanism for the CDs luminescence has not yet been fully understood, several abilities are proposed including the optical selection of nanoparticles with different sizes (also named the quantum effect), the bandgap transitions caused by the conjugated π-domains [48], or the multiple defects on its surface [49,50].

The fluorescent QY for as-synthesized NH2-CDs is estimated at room temperature under an excitation of 330 nm from a 450W Xenon lamp by comparing the integrated PL and absorbance intensities against the standard quinine sulfate based on the previously reported method [51]. The QY of quinine sulfate reported to be 54.6% when dispersed in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index n R = 1.33) was employed as a standard reference. The absorbance of quinine sulfate and the aqueous CDs solutions must be kept below 0.1 to avoid the inner filter effect [52]. The QY of as-prepared CDs (QS) is calculated according to the following equation:
(1)

where Q is the quantum yield, K is the slope value of the fitting line, and n S is the refractive index of the solvent (herein, DI water to be n S = 1.33). The subscript “S” represents the testing sample and “R” refers to the standard reference (quinine sulfate). According to equation (1), the fluorescence QY of CDs is determined to be high about 6.28% (Fig. 4b and Table 1 ), which is higher than previous reports [27,53].

The stability of CDs under different influence conditions is one of the most significant impacts to apply them in many fluorescent sensing fields. To evaluate those stable abilities, the same volume of aqueous NH2-CDs solutions (at a concentration of 50 μg mL−1) was prepared in the same quartz cuvettes sealed by glue carefully to avoid water evaporation. Subsequently, six cuvettes put under the continuous irradiation of UV light (mercury lamp, 365 nm) for the different corresponding regular intervals (15–90 min) were measured the PL intensity each of them, another one was kept undisturbed at ambient conditions in the PL dark chamber for measuring the fluorescence intensity over time (until 120 days). The results in Fig. 5 a and b shows that as-synthesized CDs are very photo-stable (remaining ∼94.5% after being irradiated for 1.5 h) and storage-stable (almost no significant reduction within 4 months). These findings imply that the long-term stability of the as-prepared NH2-CDs is relatively good, making them potentially suitable for fluorescent sensing in different conditions. To further investigate the stability of NH2-CDs by additives, the mixtures of aqueous CDs with additives were prepared so that the CDs solutions were fixed at the same concentration (50 μg mL−1) in the various amounts of NaCl (from 0.1 to 3.0 M) and the same amount (0.5 M) of different individual buffers including EDTA, Tris-HAc, HEPES, Tris-HCl, and TES. The experimental results show that the PL reduction is negligible about 3.0–3.5% after adding the different buffers (Fig. 5c and d) even at a high concentration of salt (3.0 M).

Overall, the resulting blue-emitting CDs can still emit bright and stable blue luminescence either at a high concentration of salt or the existence of additives. Additionally, they also show good photostability under continuous illumination conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effect on the fluorescent response of NH2-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-understood that the pH value of the CDs solution plays an important role as a crucial factor in the sensing mechanism [52]. To investigate the optical emission of the as-synthesized CDs with and without the existence of Cu2+ in a suitable range of pH values from 3 to 10, their fluorescent intensities with the same concentration (50 μg mL−1 for CDs and 50 μM for Cu2+) dispersed in the aqueous PBS solutions with desired pH values adjusted by adding H3PO4 or NaOH are measured by a luminescence spectrometer. Fig. 6 illustrates that the PL intensity of the CDs and CDs-Cu2+ responses is pH-dependent. For bare CDs, the PL intensity first increases slightly from 3 to 7 then gradually decreases with a further rise of pH, and the highest PL intensity is observed at 7. This phenomenon is attributed to the different protonation amounts of –NH2 groups on the CDs surface at various pH values [52]. For Cu2+-added CDs, under high acidity (pH ≤ 5), the Cu2+ addition is almost no effect on their emission responses since NH2-CDs are unable to form amine complexes with Cu2+ driven by the over-protonation of amine groups on the CDs surface in excess H+ media. Similarly, it also has a major impact in high alkaline solutions (pH ≥ 9) because Cu2+ is unstable due to the formation of copper hydroxide from OH− in the medium. The CDs-Cu2+ fluorescence alteration in a pH range of 6–8 is interesting but their maximum quenching occurs at pH = 7, implying the use of the NH2-CDs in practical trials should handle in neutral media to detect effectively copper ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in the aqueous NH2-CDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: The copper ions play an important role in daily life [1]. As the above result, the pH value of 7 is the most optimal condition to detect Cu2+ in various solutions for the applications in CDs-based Cu2+ sensors. Moreover, the sensitivity, dynamic range, and detection limit (DL) are also important parameters for sensors. To survey the sensitivity of the sensor, a definite amount of Cu2+ was added into the CDs buffer solutions where the CDs concentration in solution is constant at 50 μg mL−1 and the Cu2+ concentration raises from 0 to 400 μM in neutral media (pH = 7) along the evaluation targets. Fig. 7 a shows that CDs are sensitive to Cu2+ and the PL intensity monotonically decreases with the steady increase of Cu2+ concentration, reaching a “leveling-off” point at the dose of Cu2+ higher than 0.1 mM. The emission intensity at 430 nm is almost quenched when the concentration of Cu2+ reaches 0.4 mM with a quenching efficiency of (F0 – F)/F0 × 100 = 85.9%. The quenching mechanism has been proposed previously based on the formation of the cupric amine complex on the NH2-CDs surface [52].

The fluorescent quenching effect of CDs can be defined by the Stern-Volmer equation [52].

Fig. 7b shows the plot of (F0/F −1) vs. concentration of Cu2+, although the plot does not linearly fit from Stern-Volmer equation in the whole explored concentration range, it is well-fitted in a low concentration range from 0 to 100 μM with a determination coefficient (R2) asymptotic to 1, designating a very good linear relationship. According to equation (2), a slope of the fitting line is determined ∼0.03971 (i.e., KSV = 3.971 × 104 M−1). The DL of metal ions was calculated following by DL = 3σ/KSV [52], where σ is the standard deviation of the CDs buffer solution without Cu2+ in which this solution was measured 15 times to get the σ of ∼0.14%. Subsequently, DL for the Cu2+ detection is calculated to be 105.7 nM, which is very much lower than the maximum level of Cu2+ (∼20 μM) in drinking water following the US standard [54]. The high sensitivity with a low DL for the effective Cu2+ detection from our preparations is a useful insight for many practical applications in the sensing fields if compared to other materials (see Table 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the NH2-CDs for Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity and specificity of the NH2-CDs for the detection of a unique metal ion, in the PBS buffer solution, the PL responses of CDs (50 μg mL−1) to each metal ion (Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Co2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, and Zn2+ with the same concentration of 50 μM), each amino acid (Gly, Asp, Phe, His, Val, Thr, Ala, Cys, Trp, Leu, and Gln with the same concentration of 50 μM), the mixture of metal ions (50 μM for each ion), and the mixture of amino acids (50 μM for each acid) with the absence or presence of Cu2+ were investigated under pH = 7 at ambient condition.

Fig. 8 a shows that the PL intensities have no remarkable difference under the presence of other metallic ions except Cu2+. Interestingly, the fluorescent signal significantly decreases ∼70.5% when the addition of Cu2+ to NH2-CDs solutions with coexisting other metal cations (Fig. 8b), implying that the coexisted metallic ions did not cause any considerable noise to the Cu2+ sensing. These results exhibit the good selectivity of the as-prepared NH2-CDs in the Cu2+ detection.

Almost all amino acids do not exhibit any specific response to the fluorescence quenching of NH2-CDs at the conventional dose (Fig. 8c). Although amino acids may interfere with the recognition of Cu2+ in CDs solutions because of the complex formation between Cu2+ and its intrinsic –NH2/–NH groups, interestingly, the experiment results show that the fluorescent intensity immediately decreases ∼68.5% when the addition of Cu2+ (50 μM) into CDs even in the presence of either each amino acid (Fig. 8c) or amino-acids mixture (Fig. 8d). This phenomenon demonstrates that the high selectivity of CDs to Cu2+ against both metallic ions and amino acids has many potential applications for the detection of Cu2+ in the biological and physiological fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism of Cu2+ sensing by NH2-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that CDs are efficient electron donors and receptors [34–36]. As is seen in Fig. 7a, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared NH2-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. The presence of amine groups on the surface was a major contributor in the fluorescence quenching of Cu2+ since bare CDs without amine functionalization had no significant quenching effect compared to the amine-functionalized one [36]. Whereby, the amine groups were used to detect Cu2+ with high selectivity [34–36]. Due to a large number of amine groups dominating the surface of the as-prepared CDs, determined by the XPS (Fig. 2) and FTIR (Fig. 3a) spectra, copper ions were bound and bonded by the amine groups, leading to the formation of dendritic cupric amines, resulting in a selective quenching of the fluorescence of NH2-CDs (Scheme 2 ). It is probable to facilitate the efficient electron transfer between photo-excited NH2-CDs towards the cupric amine, causing an increase in the quenching, which is closely appropriate with the study on the Cu2+ detection of carbon dots [34–36].

These types of CDs were also suggested to be reusable and stable as the quality of their fluorescence signal did not significantly change after the incubation [27] and radiation. The capabilities of low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and cellular imaging of NH2-CDs have been also confirmed previously [27]. Furthermore, the low cost of the raw precursor, as well as the ease in the preparation of NH2-CDs, could enhance their economic viability and scalability. As a result, our as-prepared CDs provided a useful solution for efficiently detecting Cu2+. However, a larger-scale production is expected for further large-scale utilization [69].

In this work, the CDs with amino-functionalized surfaces were simultaneously designed and prepared from bulk chitosan through a simple carbonization process, which exhibited high QY (6.28% at 330 nm excitation), long photostability, good sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ as a nanosensor. The amine groups of the NH2-CDs could sensitively link with Cu2+ and form complexed dendritic cupric amines, leading to the quenching of PL intensity. The fluorescence signals were in a good linear relationship to the concentrations of Cu2+ short-ranging from 0 to 100 μM with a DL as low as 105.7 nM. The NH2-CDs demonstrated various advantages via a quick fluorescence response such as the stability, high sensitivity and selectivity, low interference, as well as a broad linear range of the Cu2+ detection, and the easy preparation. These attractive characteristics of CDs provide promising applications in the trace-Cu2+ imaging which paves the way for its in-situ Cu2+ identification on further possible biological applications.
